literal: cognition-only-ness; the thesis that what appears as object is nothing over and above cognitive presentation
invariant_definition: *Vijñapti-mātratā* is the Yogācāra thesis that the object as cognized does not require an extra-cognitive thing in order to explain its appearing. The doctrine therefore belongs with the analyzes of *ālambana*, *ālaya-vijñāna*, *vāsanā*, and *trisvabhāva*. It is not a casual claim that "everything is mind" in the modern idealist sense. Its real target is the reification of a subject over here and an independently self-grounded object over there. The appearance of that duality is explained from within the cognitive stream itself, through karmically perfumed dispositions maturing in ordered ways. This is why Yogācāra appeals to dream, hallucination, and shared karmic conditioning, and why critics press the objections from spatial regularity, temporal regularity, intersubjective convergence, and practical efficacy. In contemplative use, the doctrine is not merely theoretical: it undercuts grasping at the *grāhya-grāhaka* split by showing that the split is constructed within dependent cognition itself.

per_school.definition: The thesis is defended through the claim that what appears as external object is a manifestation of cognitive transformation. The standard realist objections are answered by appeal to shared karmic seeds, so that apparently public and stable worlds can arise without positing independently existing external objects. The doctrine is inseparable from the *trisvabhāva* framework: the duality taken as external is *parikalpita*; the dependently arisen flow is *paratantra*; the non-dual realization of that flow is *pariniṣpanna*.

Mādhyamaka objects that Yogācāra has only relocated *svabhāva* from the external object to cognition itself. A metaphysical claim that cognition alone truly exists is still a thesis about what has intrinsic status, and therefore remains vulnerable to the same critique that destroyed external-object realism. The Mādhyamika will accept the deconstruction of naive realism while refusing the positive elevation of cognition into a final ontological ground.

Vedānta rejects *vijñapti-mātratā* because cognition, on its own, does not explain the determinacy and resistance of the world. The stock reply is that dreams cannot serve as the model for waking experience unless waking experience is already granted its own distinct standing. Even Advaita, which grants the world a non-ultimate status, does not accept the Yogācāra elimination of the external object at the level of ordinary practice.

translator_note: "Consciousness-only," "cognition-only," and "representation-only" each catch part of the doctrine and miss part of it. "Mind-only" is especially lazy when used without explanation: it imports modern idealist baggage and obscures the technical dispute over cognitive presentation, objecthood, and karmic regularity. The term is better left partly untranslated in serious use, then explained through its links to *ālaya-vijñāna*, *vāsanā*, and the *grāhya-grāhaka* critique.
